question: Your tee shot lands in a yellow penalty area (water hazard). What are your options?
ground_truth: **Rule 17.1d** - "The player may play the original ball or another ball from where the previous stroke was made" and "keeping the estimated point where the original ball last crossed the edge of the penalty area between the hole and the spot where the ball is dropped" **Ruling**: You have two relief options from a yellow penalty area, each with a one-stroke penalty. **Penalties/Procedures**: (1) Stroke-and-distance relief: Play from where your last stroke was made, adding one penalty stroke. (2) Back-on-the-line relief: Drop outside the penalty area keeping the crossing point between the hole and drop spot, adding one penalty stroke. Unlike red penalty areas, lateral relief is not available. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-17
response:
RULING: Since your tee shot landed in a yellow penalty area, you have several options under the rules:

You may:
1) Play the ball as it lies in the penalty area, with no penalty. You can ground your club and take practice swings in the penalty area.
2) Take relief by dropping a ball within two club-lengths of and not nearer the hole than the point where the ball last crossed the edge of the penalty area, with a one-stroke penalty.
3) Take relief by dropping a ball on the opposite side of the penalty area equidistant from the hole, with a one-stroke penalty.
4) Go back to the teeing area and play under stroke and distance, with a one-stroke penalty.

The rule governing penalty areas (which replaced water hazards) provides these options. I would need to know the specific details of your ball's position and lie within the penalty area to advise further on the best relief option for you.

question: You hit your tee shot and it goes out of bounds. What must you do?
ground_truth: **Rule 18** - "the player must take stroke-and-distance relief by adding one penalty stroke and playing the original ball or another ball from where the previous stroke was made" **Ruling**: When your ball goes out of bounds, you must take stroke-and-distance relief. **Penalties/Procedures**: Add one penalty stroke to your score and play another ball from the tee (where the previous stroke was made). A ball is out of bounds only when all of it is outside the boundary edge of the course. The original ball must be abandoned once you put another ball into play. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-18
response:
RULING: When your tee shot goes out of bounds, you must proceed under the stroke-and-distance procedure.

This means you must go back and play another shot from the teeing area, adding one penalty stroke to your score. So your next stroke will be your fourth shot (your original tee shot, plus one penalty stroke).

The rule governing out of bounds is the rule on stroke and distance relief. Since your ball went out of bounds, you have the option to play a ball from where you last played a stroke (the teeing area), adding one penalty stroke. This is your only relief option in this situation.

question: Your ball is in a greenside bunker. Can you remove a small stone lying next to your ball before playing your shot?
ground_truth: **Rule 12.2a** - "Before playing a ball in a bunker, a player may remove loose impediments under Rule 15.1 and movable obstructions under Rule 15.2" **Ruling**: Yes, you can remove loose impediments like stones from bunkers without penalty. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty for removing stones or other loose impediments from bunkers. Incidental contact with sand during removal is permitted. However, you cannot deliberately touch sand to test conditions or during practice swings (Rule 12.2b - general penalty applies). **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-12
response:
RULING: Yes, you may remove the small stone lying next to your ball in the bunker.

The current Rules of Golf allow players to touch or move loose impediments, including stones, in a bunker. As long as you are not testing the condition of the sand or touching sand in the area right behind or in front of the ball, you are permitted to remove the small stone before playing your shot.

question: Your ball comes to rest on a paved cart path in the fairway. Are you entitled to free relief?
ground_truth: **Rule 16.1** - "You are only allowed to take free relief from a cart path if it is artificially surfaced" **Ruling**: Yes, you are entitled to free relief from an artificially surfaced (paved) cart path, which is classified as an immovable obstruction. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. Locate the nearest point where you no longer have interference, drop within one club-length of that point not nearer the hole. The ball must stay in the same area of the course. Natural grass or sand paths do not qualify for relief. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/articles/taking-free-relief-under-the-rules
response:
RULING: Yes, you are entitled to free relief from the paved cart path.

The rules governing interference from an abnormal course condition, which includes paved cart paths, allow you to take free relief. Since your ball is on the paved cart path in the fairway, you may take relief without penalty by dropping your ball within one club-length of the nearest point of complete relief, no nearer the hole.

The relevant rule is the one governing interference from abnormal course conditions. This allows free relief when your ball is in, on, or touches an abnormal course condition, and the condition interferes with your intended stroke.

question: Your ball is on the putting green. Can you repair spike marks in your line of putt?
ground_truth: **Rule 13.1c** - "Damage on the putting green includes shoe damage (such as spike marks) and scrapes or indentations caused by equipment or a flagstick" **Ruling**: Yes, you can repair spike marks on the putting green, including in your line of putt. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty for repairing spike marks. Use your hand, foot, body part, ball-mark repair tool, tee, club or similar equipment. Must not unreasonably delay play. Cannot repair natural surface imperfections, aeration holes, or damage from normal maintenance practices. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-13
response:
RULING: Yes, you may repair spike marks or other damage on the putting green that might interfere with the line of your putt.

The rule governing repairs on the putting green allows you to repair any damage, including spike marks, that might interfere with the line of play. This is covered under the rule governing "Allowed and Prohibited Actions on the Putting Green." There is no penalty for repairing such damage.

question: In match play, you and your opponent both make a 5 on a hole. What is the result of the hole?
ground_truth: **Rule 3.2a(2)** - "The player and opponent complete the hole in the same number of strokes (including strokes made and penalty strokes)" **Ruling**: The hole is halved (tied). **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. When both players complete a hole with the same score, the hole is halved and neither player wins the hole. Players can also agree to halve a hole after at least one player has made a stroke to begin the hole. The match status remains unchanged for that hole. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-3
response:
RULING: The hole is halved in match play.

In match play, when both players make the same score on a hole, the hole is considered "halved" and the match remains tied for that hole. There is no winner or loser of the hole when the scores are equal.

This is different from stroke play, where equal scores would simply be added to each player's total. But in match play, the objective is to win more holes than your opponent, not shoot the lowest total score. So a halved hole is the result when you both make the same score.

question: You hit your ball into thick rough and cannot find it. How long do you have to search before it is considered lost?
ground_truth: **Rule 18.2a(1)** - "A ball is lost if not found in three minutes after the player or their caddie begins to search for it" **Ruling**: You have three minutes to search for your ball before it is considered lost. **Penalties/Procedures**: If not found in three minutes, the ball is lost and you must take stroke-and-distance relief by returning to where your previous stroke was made and adding one penalty stroke. If found within three minutes but identity is uncertain, you get reasonable additional time to identify it. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-18
response:
RULING: The ball is considered lost if it is not found within 3 minutes after the player or the player's caddie or partner begin searching for it.

The rule governing lost ball is the rule on ball search. You are allowed to search for a ball that may be lost for up to 3 minutes. If the ball is not found within that 3-minute time period, the ball is deemed lost and the player must proceed under the lost ball rule.

To proceed under the lost ball rule, the player has two options:
1) Play a ball under stroke and distance, going back to where the previous stroke was made and adding a penalty stroke.
2) Take back-on-the-line relief, dropping a ball within two club-lengths of the spot where the ball is estimated to have last crossed the edge of the area where it is known or virtually certain to be lost, and taking a one-stroke penalty.

The 3-minute search time is strictly applied and measured from when the player, caddie, or partner begins searching. The player may not play a provisional ball until after the 3 minutes have elapsed and the original ball is determined to be lost.

question: In match play, your putt from the green hits the flagstick that was left in the hole. Is there a penalty?
response:
RULING: There is no penalty if a player's putt from the putting green hits the flagstick that has been left in the hole.

In match play, Rule 13.2a(2) allows a player to make a stroke with the flagstick left in the hole. There is no penalty if the ball hits the flagstick. This is different from the penalty that would apply in stroke play.

question: In match play, your opponent concedes your next putt. Can you refuse the concession and putt anyway?
response:
RULING: No, in match play a concession cannot be declined or withdrawn.

In match play, a player may concede a stroke, a hole, or the match at any time. Once a player has conceded the next stroke, the player's turn is over and the concession must be accepted - it cannot be refused. The opponent is not required to play out the conceded stroke.

This is a key difference from stroke play, where a player must complete each hole even if an opponent or fellow-competitor offers to concede a putt or hole. In match play, concessions are binding and final.

question: You think your tee shot might be lost in the woods. You announce you will play a provisional ball and hit another. Your original is then found in the fairway. Which ball do you play?
response:
RULING: You play the original ball found in the fairway.

Since the original ball was found within the 3-minute search time, the provisional ball you played must be abandoned. You must continue play with the original ball, which is now your ball in play.

When a player plays a provisional ball after a tee shot, the provisional only becomes the ball in play if the original ball is either lost (not found within 3 minutes) or known or virtually certain to be out of bounds. Since the original ball was found within the search time in the fairway, the provisional is no longer needed and must be abandoned.

question: You take a free drop from an immovable obstruction. You drop the ball from knee height and it bounces and rolls outside the one-club-length relief area. What must you do?
response:
RULING: You must re-drop the ball within the relief area.

When taking relief from an immovable obstruction, you must drop the ball within the one-club-length relief area. If the ball bounces and rolls outside that area after being dropped, the ball must be re-dropped within the relief area. This is according to the procedure for re-dropping a ball under the current Rules of Golf.

question: Your ball is unplayable in a bunker. You decide to take back-on-the-line relief. Must you drop the ball inside the bunker or can you drop outside it?
response:
RULING: You may drop the ball outside the bunker when taking back-on-the-line relief for an unplayable ball in a bunker.

The rule governing unplayable ball relief allows the player to take relief by dropping a ball under the back-on-the-line option. When doing so, the reference point is the estimated point right behind the ball where the ball lies, and the ball must be dropped within one club-length of that reference point, not nearer the hole. The ball may be dropped either inside or outside the bunker, as long as it ends up within one club-length of the reference point and not nearer the hole.

The key is that the unplayable ball relief procedure allows the player to drop the ball outside the bunker if desired, as an alternative to dropping inside the bunker. This can be a helpful option if the conditions inside the bunker are particularly difficult or the player wants to play from a different lie.

question: Your ball lies in the general area just outside a GUR boundary, but your stance would be inside the GUR. Are you entitled to free relief?
response:
RULING: Yes, you are entitled to free relief from the GUR.

Even though your ball lies outside the GUR boundary, since your stance would be inside the GUR, the rule governing abnormal course conditions applies. This allows you to take free relief, dropping the ball within one club-length of the nearest point of complete relief, no nearer the hole.

The key is that the abnormal course condition (the GUR) interferes with your stance, even though it does not directly interfere with the ball's lie. This is covered under the rule describing abnormal course conditions and the procedures for taking relief.

question: While searching for your ball in the rough, you accidentally kick it. What is the penalty and what must you do?
response:
RULING: There is no penalty for accidentally kicking your ball in the rough. You must replace the ball on its original spot.

The rule governing this situation is the rule for "Ball Moved by Player" in the general area (anywhere except the teeing area, penalty area, bunker, or putting green). Since you accidentally moved the ball, with no intent to improve your lie or position, there is no penalty. You simply need to replace the ball on its original spot before playing your next stroke.

This is different from the rule for a ball moved on the putting green, where the ball must always be replaced regardless of how it was moved. But in the general area, unintentional ball movement by the player results in no penalty, just a replacement.

question: In match play, you play out of turn from the tee. Can your opponent make you replay the shot?
response:
RULING: In match play, if a player makes a stroke before it is their turn to play, the opponent may require the player to replay the stroke.

The order of play in match play is very important. If a player plays out of turn from the teeing area, the opponent has the option to require the player to replay the stroke in the correct order. This is different from stroke play, where there is no penalty for playing out of turn.

The opponent does not have to allow the out-of-turn stroke to stand. They can invoke their right to have the player replay the shot in the proper order.

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. You find your ball sitting on dry land with a playable lie. Are you required to take a penalty drop, or can you play the ball as it lies?
response:
RULING: You may play the ball as it lies in the penalty area without penalty.

The current Rules of Golf allow you to play a ball from a red penalty area without taking any penalty strokes. Since your ball is sitting on dry land within the penalty area boundaries and has a playable lie, you may play the ball as it lies. There is no requirement to take relief with a penalty drop.

The previous distinction between "water hazards" and "lateral water hazards" no longer exists. All such areas are now categorized as either red or yellow penalty areas under the 2019 Rules of Golf revision. The key difference is that in a red penalty area, you have the option to play the ball as it lies without penalty, whereas in a yellow penalty area, you must take a one-stroke penalty to play the ball as it lies or proceed under another relief option.

So in this situation, with your ball in a red penalty area, you may play it as it lies without penalty.

question: A movable obstruction (a rake) is lying in a bunker. Your ball is not in the bunker but the rake is on your line of play. Can you move the rake?
response:
RULING: Yes, you may remove the movable obstruction (the rake) from your line of play in the bunker.

The rules allow you to remove any movable obstruction, including objects like rakes, that are on your line of play, even if the obstruction is in a bunker. This is true regardless of whether your ball is in the bunker or not. You may move the rake without penalty.

Once the rake is removed, you must be careful not to improve your conditions for the stroke, such as by testing the sand. But you are allowed to take the stroke with the obstruction removed from your intended line.

question: You start your round with 14 clubs. On the 5th hole you discover that one of the clubs belongs to another player and was placed in your bag by mistake. How many clubs do you have for the purpose of the 14-club limit?
response:
RULING: You have 13 clubs for the purpose of the 14-club limit.

When a player begins a round with more than 14 clubs and discovers this during the round, the player must immediately remove the excess club(s) from their bag. The player is penalized 2 strokes for having more than 14 clubs, but there is no additional penalty for each hole played with the extra club(s).

So in this case, you must remove the 15th club (the one that belongs to another player) from your bag. For the rest of the round, you will be playing with 13 clubs, which is within the 14-club limit.

The rules governing the 14-club limit are found in the description of Rule 4.1b, which covers the maximum number of clubs allowed.

question: In match play, you realize on the 4th tee that you have been playing with 15 clubs since the start of the round. What is the penalty?
response:
RULING: In match play, the penalty for carrying more than 14 clubs is loss of hole for each hole in which the player has more than 14 clubs, up to a maximum of two holes.

Since you realized the excess club on the 4th tee, the penalty applies for holes 1-4. The opponent wins the first two holes, and the match continues from there.

The rules governing the maximum of 14 clubs are found in the rule covering clubs. This situation does not involve a situation where the players can agree to ignore the rules.

question: Your ball is embedded in the face of a bunker, above the sand, in a grassy area that is part of the bunker wall. Are you entitled to free relief for an embedded ball?
response:
RULING: Yes, the player is entitled to free relief for an embedded ball in this situation.

The rule governing embedded balls (Rule 16.3) states that a player may take free relief if their ball is embedded in its own pitch-mark in any part of the general area, which includes the sandy area of a bunker as well as the grass face of the bunker walls. Since the ball is embedded above the sand in the bunker wall, the player can take relief under this rule.

The player should mark the spot of the embedded ball, lift it, and then drop the ball within one club-length of that spot, not nearer the hole. The ball must be dropped in the general area. The player is entitled to this free relief, as the ball is embedded in its own pitch-mark in a part of the bunker that is considered the general area.

question: You play a provisional ball from the tee. Your provisional lands in a red penalty area. Before searching for your original, you play a second provisional from the tee. Is the second provisional valid?
response:
RULING: The second provisional ball played from the tee is valid.

When a player's original ball might be lost outside a penalty area or might be out of bounds, the player may play a provisional ball under the rules governing provisional balls. Since your first provisional landed in a red penalty area, the rules allow you to continue playing additional provisional balls from the teeing area until you have either found your original ball or exceeded the three-minute time limit to search for it. The second provisional you played is valid and in play.

The key factors here are:
- The original ball was not known or virtually certain to be in the penalty area when the first provisional was played.
- You played the second provisional from the same teeing area as the first, which is permitted.
- You have not exceeded the three-minute time limit to search for the original ball.

So the second provisional is a valid ball in play under the rules.

question: Your ball is at rest on the putting green. A strong gust of wind moves it into a bunker. Must you play it from the bunker or can you replace it on the green?
response:
RULING: The ball must be replaced on its original spot on the putting green.

Under the current Rules of Golf, if a ball at rest on the putting green is moved by natural forces such as wind, it must be replaced on its original spot, with no penalty. This is different from a ball that is moved by natural forces elsewhere on the course, which is played from its new position.

Since the ball on the putting green was moved by the wind into the bunker, you must replace it on its original spot on the green, with no penalty.

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. You decide to take lateral relief, but the nearest point within two club-lengths is on the other side of the penalty area. Can you drop there?
response:
RULING: No, you cannot drop on the other side of the penalty area when taking lateral relief.

The rule governing relief from penalty areas states that when taking lateral relief, the reference point for dropping the ball is the nearest point of complete relief within two club-lengths of the original spot, but not nearer the hole. This point must be in the general area, which does not include the penalty area itself. Since the nearest point of relief is on the other side of the penalty area, that is not a valid relief option.

To take lateral relief from a red penalty area, you must find a point within two club-lengths on your side of the penalty area that provides complete relief. If there is no such point, your only other option is to take stroke-and-distance relief by going back to where you last played from.

question: In match play, you and your opponent both play wrong balls on a par 3. You each realize the mistake before teeing off on the next hole. What is the ruling?
response:
RULING: In match play, if both players play a wrong ball, the result is that the hole is halved.

The rules governing wrong ball in match play are:

- If a player plays a wrong ball, the penalty is loss of the hole. 
- However, if both players play a wrong ball on the same hole, the hole is simply halved. There is no penalty to either player.

Since you and your opponent both realized the mistake before starting the next hole, the proper procedure is to simply halve the hole. The match continues from there, with no penalty to either player.

The key difference from stroke play is that in match play, playing a wrong ball results in loss of the hole, rather than a two-stroke penalty as in stroke play. But when both players make the same mistake, the hole is just halved.

question: Your ball is in the general area and an immovable obstruction (a sprinkler head) interferes with your stance. However, the obstruction is inside a red penalty area while your ball is outside it. Can you take free relief from the obstruction?
response:
RULING: No, you cannot take free relief from the immovable obstruction in this case.

Even though the obstruction interferes with your stance, since it is located inside the penalty area while your ball is outside the penalty area, the rule governing relief from immovable obstructions does not apply. You must proceed under the rules for the penalty area.

The rules for penalty areas take precedence in this situation. Since your ball is outside the penalty area, you do not have the option to take free relief from the obstruction that is inside the penalty area. Your only options are to play the ball as it lies, take relief from the penalty area by dropping a ball under the applicable penalty area rules, or declare your ball unplayable and take an unplayable ball relief option.

question: Your ball is plugged in the steep face of a bunker. You declare it unplayable and choose lateral relief (two club-lengths). All points within two club-lengths that are not nearer the hole are outside the bunker in the general area. Where must you drop?
response:
RULING: You must drop the ball in the general area outside the bunker, not nearer the hole than the nearest point of relief, within two club-lengths of that point.

When your ball is plugged in the face of a bunker and you declare it unplayable, you have the option to take lateral relief. This means you may drop the ball within two club-lengths of the nearest point of relief that is not nearer the hole, but the relief area must be outside the bunker, in the general area. You cannot take relief by dropping the ball back in the bunker, even if it's within two club-lengths.

The key rules governing this are:
- The rule on unplayable ball (Rule 19)
- The rule on lateral relief from a bunker (Rule 19.2b)
- The definition of "general area" (which is everywhere on the course except the teeing area, penalty areas, bunkers, and the putting green)

So in this case, you must find the nearest point of relief outside the bunker, then drop within two club-lengths of that point, not nearer the hole.

question: In stroke play, you putt from on the green and your ball is still moving when it hits another player's ball that was at rest on the green. What happens?
response:
RULING: When a ball in motion accidentally hits another ball at rest on the putting green, the player whose ball was struck incurs a one-stroke penalty, and the ball must be replaced on its original spot.

The relevant rule is the one governing "Ball Striking Another Ball" on the putting green. Since the balls were both on the putting green, Rule 11.1 applies. Under Rule 11.1a, if a player's ball in motion accidentally hits another ball at rest on the putting green, the player whose ball was struck gets a one-stroke penalty, and the struck ball must be replaced on its original spot.

The player whose ball was in motion does not incur any penalty. Their ball is played from where it comes to rest after striking the other ball.

question: In match play, you are 3 down with 3 holes to play. On the 16th tee, your opponent concedes the hole. On the 17th, you win the hole. You are now 1 down with 1 to play. On the 18th green, your opponent concedes your birdie putt, tying the match. Your opponent then says they want to withdraw the concession on hole 16 because they made it by mistake. Can they?
response:
RULING: No, the opponent's concession of the 16th hole cannot be withdrawn.

In match play, a concession, whether of a stroke, hole, or the match, is binding and cannot be declined or withdrawn once made. The opponent's concession of the 16th hole stands, even if they later claim it was a mistake.

Once a hole has been completed, the result cannot be changed, even if a rules violation is later discovered. The only way to address a potential rules issue is to raise it before either player makes a stroke on the next hole. 

Since the 16th hole was completed and the 17th hole was played out after the concession, the opponent cannot now go back and undo the concession. The match is tied after 18 holes.

question: Your ball is in the general area and you have interference from an animal hole. The nearest point of complete relief would be on a putting green (not the green of the hole being played). Where do you take relief?
response:
RULING: You may take relief from the interference of the animal hole, but you cannot take relief that results in your ball being placed on a putting green other than the green of the hole you are playing.

The rule governing interference from an abnormal course condition, such as an animal hole, allows you to take free relief. The nearest point of complete relief must be in the general area, not on a putting green. You would drop the ball in the general area, keeping the point where the ball is estimated to have come to rest between you and the hole, no nearer the hole. You cannot use the relief procedure to place your ball on any putting green other than the one for the hole you are playing.

question: In match play, you hit your approach shot and it lands on the green. Your opponent, who hasn't played yet, picks up your ball thinking it's theirs. They realize the mistake and replace it. What is the penalty?
response:
RULING: In match play, if a player lifts the opponent's ball on the putting green, the penalty is loss of the hole.

Since this occurred on the putting green, and the opponent lifted the player's ball thinking it was their own, the penalty is loss of the hole to the opponent under the rule governing lifting the opponent's ball on the putting green in match play.

The opponent was required to replace the lifted ball on its original spot. No further penalty applies.

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. Before playing your next stroke, you ground your club in the penalty area and take a practice swing that touches the ground. What is the penalty?
response:
RULING: There is no penalty for grounding your club or taking a practice swing in a penalty area before playing your next stroke.

The current Rules of Golf allow players to ground their club and take practice swings in penalty areas without penalty. This was a change made in the 2019 rules revision - prior to that, it would have been a one-stroke penalty for touching the ground in a water hazard (now called a penalty area).

So in this scenario, you have not incurred any penalty for grounding your club or taking the practice swing in the red penalty area. You may proceed to play your next stroke from within the penalty area.

question: You take relief from a cart path. You determine the nearest point of complete relief and drop within one club-length. The ball comes to rest in a position where you now have interference from a sprinkler head. Must you play it as it lies, or can you take additional relief from the sprinkler head?
response:
RULING: You may take free relief from the interference caused by the sprinkler head.

When you took relief from the cart path, you were entitled to find the nearest point of complete relief and drop your ball within one club-length of that spot, no nearer the hole. However, that drop has now resulted in your ball being in a position where you have interference from a different condition, the sprinkler head. 

Under the rules governing abnormal course conditions, which include sprinkler heads, you are allowed to take free relief. You can find the nearest point of complete relief from the interference of the sprinkler head and drop your ball within one club-length of that spot, no nearer the hole. You are not limited to just the original relief area, but can take this additional relief.

The rules allow you to take all relief needed to address interference from any abnormal course condition, such as the sprinkler head in this case, even if it means taking multiple relief drops. You are not required to just play the ball as it lies if it ends up in another impacted area.

question: In match play, on the final hole, both players are on the green. Player A is 20 feet away, Player B is 6 feet away. Player A putts first and misses, leaving a 2-foot putt. Player B then concedes Player A's next putt. Player A picks up their ball. Player B then three-putts from 6 feet. Player A claims they win the hole because Player B made 5 and their conceded putt means they made 4. Player B says the hole was halved because the concession happened before B finished. Who is correct?
response:
RULING: Player A is correct. The hole is won by Player A.

In match play, a player may concede a stroke, a hole, or the match at any time. Once a concession is made, it cannot be declined or withdrawn. 

When Player B conceded Player A's next putt, that concession was immediate and binding. Therefore, Player A's score for the hole is 4, while Player B's score is 5. The hole is won by Player A.

The fact that Player B then three-putted from 6 feet is irrelevant, as the outcome of the hole was already determined by the concession. Player B's three-putt does not change the result.

The key points are:
- Concessions in match play are immediately effective and cannot be taken back
- Player A's score for the hole is 4 due to the concession, while Player B's is 5
- This results in Player A winning the hole, regardless of what happened after the concession

question: Your ball is in a bunker. Before your stroke, you smooth footprints in the bunker that are 10 feet away from your ball and not on your line of play. You do this to care for the course. Is there a penalty?
response:
RULING: There is no penalty for smoothing the sand in the bunker, as long as you do not touch the sand in the area right behind or in front of your ball.

Under the current Rules of Golf, players are allowed to touch or smooth the sand in a bunker, as long as they are not testing the condition of the bunker or improving the lie of their ball. Smoothing footprints or other irregularities in the sand that are away from your ball's position is permitted. The rules specifically allow players to "smooth sand or soil in the bunker with a hand or club" without penalty.

The key is that the smoothing must be done away from the area right behind or in front of the ball. If you had touched the sand in that vicinity, it would be a penalty under the rule governing "testing the condition of the bunker." But since you were caring for the course in an area away from your ball, there is no penalty.

question: In stroke play, you hit your tee shot into the woods. You don't play a provisional. After searching for 2 minutes, your playing partner finds a ball. You identify it as yours but it's in an unplayable lie. You declare it unplayable and take lateral relief, dropping within two club-lengths. After you play your next shot, you discover the ball you found was actually not yours (the markings were similar). What is the ruling?
response:
RULING: Since you incorrectly took relief under the unplayable ball rule for a ball that was not yours, you must return to the spot where you last played from and take stroke-and-distance relief for your original ball that is still lost.

You did not play a provisional ball before searching for the original, and the ball you found and took relief for turned out not to be yours. When a player's ball is lost (not found within 3 minutes of searching) and a provisional ball was not played, the correct procedure is to go back and play under stroke and distance from the spot of the previous stroke.

The rule governing this scenario is the one covering a ball that is lost or out of bounds. Since you did not play a provisional, you must return to the spot of your previous shot and play again under penalty of stroke and distance. The relief you took for the unplayable ball that was not yours does not apply, and that stroke along with the subsequent shot must be canceled.

question: In match play, your opponent's tee shot goes into a red penalty area. They drop under penalty of one stroke and hit their third shot onto the green. You then notice that when they dropped, they dropped on the wrong side of the penalty area (the side closer to the hole). You say nothing until after they two-putt for a 5. You then raise the issue. Is your opponent's score valid?
response:
RULING: Your opponent's score of 5 for the hole is not valid. The incorrect drop position provides the opponent with an unfair advantage, so the proper procedure must be followed.

In match play, a player must correct any known rules breaches before making a stroke to begin the next hole. Since you noticed the incorrect drop position before your opponent made a stroke on the next hole, you can raise the issue and the correct procedure must be applied.

The rule governing penalty areas states that when taking relief, the player must drop the ball in the correct relief area, which is one club-length from the point where the original ball last crossed the edge of the penalty area, not nearer the hole. By dropping on the incorrect side, closer to the hole, the opponent gained an advantage.

The proper procedure is:
- Your opponent must go back and take relief properly, re-dropping the ball in the correct relief area one club-length from where the ball last crossed the penalty area edge, not nearer the hole.
- Your opponent's score for the hole is then adjusted to account for the correct drop procedure.

The original 5 is cancelled, and your opponent must complete the hole under penalty of one stroke from the correct drop position. This may result in a higher score than the original 5.

question: Your ball is on a steep slope in the general area. You carefully place your club behind the ball to address it, and the ball moves. Under the current Rules of Golf (2023), are you deemed to have caused the ball to move?
response:
RULING: No, you are not deemed to have caused the ball to move in this situation.

The current Rules of Golf state that if a ball at rest is moved by natural forces such as wind, water, or the player's actions in making a reasonable effort to address the ball, there is no penalty and the ball must be replaced on its original spot (Rule 9.3).

Since you were carefully placing your club behind the ball in the normal process of addressing it on a steep slope, and the ball then moved as a result, you are not considered to have caused the ball to move. The ball must simply be replaced on its original position without penalty.

question: Your ball lands in a flower bed adjacent to the clubhouse. The flower bed is not marked as GUR and there is no local rule about it. Your ball is playable. A committee member walking by tells you that flower beds are always treated as GUR. Is the committee member correct?
response:
RULING: No, the committee member is incorrect. Since the flower bed is not marked as a ground under repair (GUR) area, and there is no local rule declaring it as such, the ball is playable as it lies.

The current Rules of Golf do not automatically treat flower beds as ground under repair. GUR is defined as any part of the course that the committee has marked or indicated should be avoided for play. Without the area being specifically marked or a local rule stating it is GUR, the flower bed does not qualify as such, and the ball must be played as it lies.

The player should play the ball from the flower bed, taking care to avoid damaging the plants. The player is not entitled to free relief from the flower bed in this situation.

question: Your ball is unplayable in a penalty area. Can you declare it unplayable and take unplayable ball relief (back-on-the-line or two club-lengths) under Rule 19?
response:
RULING: Yes, you can declare your ball unplayable and take unplayable ball relief if your ball is in a penalty area.

Under the rule governing unplayable ball in a penalty area, if your ball is in a penalty area, you have the option to declare it unplayable and take relief by:

1) Going back on a straight line from the hole through the spot where the ball lay, dropping a ball and taking a one-stroke penalty. This is called "back-on-the-line" relief.

2) Dropping a ball within two club-lengths of the spot where the ball lay, not nearer the hole, and taking a one-stroke penalty. This is called "two club-lengths" relief.

You do not have the option to take unplayable ball relief outside the penalty area (lateral relief or back-on-the-line outside the penalty area), as that would be considered relief from a penalty area, not unplayable ball relief.

The key is that you have the unplayable ball option when your ball is in a penalty area. This allows you to take relief under the unplayable ball rule rather than having to take penalty area relief.

question: Preferred lies (lift, clean, and place) are in effect via local rule. Your ball is in the first cut of rough just off the fairway. Can you lift, clean, and place your ball under the preferred lies local rule?
response:
RULING: Yes, you may lift, clean, and place your ball under the preferred lies local rule.

Since a local rule for preferred lies is in effect, you are allowed to lift your ball from the first cut of rough just off the fairway, clean it, and place it within one club-length of its original spot, no nearer the hole. This is permitted even though your ball was in the rough rather than on the fairway. The preferred lies local rule overrides the normal requirement to play the ball as it lies in the general area (the area of the course other than the teeing area, penalty areas, bunkers, and the putting green).
